false, "scrollable-vertical": false, "children": [{"text": "Articles like \"the\" and \"a\" appear everywhere.  In fact, they are some of the most common words in the English language. But do they really do anything? Try saying a few sentences without the articles. It might sound funny, but you\u2019ll find that it doesn\u2019t really change the meaning. In Chinese, there are no articles. For example, whether you want to say \"I am the cat\" or \"I am a cat,\" you would use \"\u6211\u662f\u732b(w\u01d2 sh\u00ec m\u0101o)\", which directly translates as \"I am cat.\" This may seem wishy-washy\u2014sometimes it really does matter whether you are the cat or a cat, but you can almost always tell from context alone which is the case.", "resource-id": "com.hellochinese:id/content", 1383, 2392], "visibility": "visible", "content-desc": [null], "rel-bounds": [0, 3748, 1326, 5097], "focused": false, "selected": false, "scrollable-vertical": false, "children": [{"text": "You might think some of our translations are a bit clumsy or don\u2019t exactly sound like something a native speaker would say. In fact, we try to style the translations (within bounds, of course) so that they are most helpful when learning Chinese, which doesn\u2019t always correspond to the most elegant phrasing. For example, \"\u6211\u8981\u559d\u4e00\u7897\u6c64(w\u01d2 y\u00e0o h\u0113 y\u012b w\u01cen t\u0101ng)\" could translate to \"I want to eat soup,\" but given the English alone you might not be able to form the Chinese sentence. \"I want to drink a bowl of soup\" may be a bit of an awkward sentence, but it\u2019s much more helpful when trying to come up with the Chinese translation.", false, "scrollable-vertical": false, "children": [{"text": "How do Chinese people greet each other? It\u2019s actually not as simple as you might think. The obvious answers is \"\u4f60\u597d(n\u01d0h\u01ceo)\", but this is a bit formal, almost always used when you are first meeting someone. An older neighbor might greet you in the morning with \"\u4f60\u5403\u4e86\u5417(n\u01d0 ch\u012b le ma)\"?, which literally means \"Have you eaten?\" For students and young adults, there\u2019s no real equivalent for \"what\u2019s up?\" Chinese people almost never ask \"\u4f60\u597d\u5417(n\u01d0 h\u01ceo ma)?\". Instead, friends often greet each other with a simple \"\u563f(h\u0113i)\" (a transliteration of \"hey\") followed by the person\u2019s name, as in \"\u563fJohn!\" Also common are \"\u65e9\u4e0a\u597d(z\u01ceosh\u00e0ng h\u01ceo, good morning)\" and \"\u665a\u4e0a\u597d(w\u01censh\u00e0ng h\u01ceo, good evening)\", depending on the time of day.", "resource-id":
